questions.prompt: Your client ranks #1 for their target keyword in your office, but their actual organic traffic from that keyword is falling. What's the most likely explanation?

choices.text: Rank monitoring from one location and one device misses how the SERP varies — and SERP features (AI Overviews, Featured Snippets, video carousels, Local Packs) are increasingly pushing the #1 organic result below the fold, absorbing clicks.

questions.explanation: Rank is not a single number. It varies by location, device, language, and personalization. And the SERP layout itself is changing fast — even a real #1 organic position can get buried beneath SERP features that absorb most of the clicks. Always monitor rank from multiple realistic contexts and look at what the SERP actually looks like.

questions.prompt: A user in Hong Kong and a user in Paris both search 'bicycle repair shops' at the same time. Which is true?

choices.text: They see the same results — Google is location-agnostic.
They see different results because location is a serving signal, and queries with local intent trigger Map results scoped to the user's area.
Only logged-in users get personalized results.
Hong Kong sees results in Chinese, Paris sees results in French — that's the only difference.

questions.explanation: Google explicitly uses this example in its documentation. Serving (Stage 3) factors in location, language, device, and many other signals — and for local-intent queries the SERP itself swaps in a Local Pack. The version of the SERP a user sees is genuinely different per user, not just translated.
